response: To solve this problem we must first select three distinct rows out of the six available rows on the board which can be done in 20 ways. Next we must select three distinct columns out of the six available columns which can also be done in 20 ways. Once the rows and columns are chosen we place the three indistinguishable rooks in the intersections of these selected rows and columns such that no two rooks share a row or column. Since the rooks are indistinguishable the number of ways to arrange them in the chosen grid is simply one unique configuration for any set of three rows and three columns. Therefore the total number of arrangements is the product of the ways to choose the rows and the ways to choose the columns. Multiplying 20 by 20 gives us a final result of 400 valid arrangements.
